title: Clean Plastics
body: Always rinse your plastic milk cartons. Leftover liquid contaminates the whole batch!

title: No Plastic Bags
body: Do not put your recycling in plastic bags; most centers cannot process them.

title: Flatten Cardboard
body: Break down cardboard boxes to save space in the recycling bin and transport trucks.

title: Check Local Rules
body: Recycling rules vary by city. Check your local council's website for specific bin colors.

title: Remove Lids
body: Plastic bottle caps are often a different plastic type. Remove them unless stated otherwise.

title: Dry Paper
body: Wet paper cannot be recycled as the fibers break down. Keep your paper bin covered.

title: E-Waste Disposal
body: Never throw batteries or old phones in the trash; they contain toxic chemicals.

title: Glass Colors
body: Most centers can recycle clear, green, and brown glass together, but check first.

title: Avoid Shredded Paper
body: Small scraps of paper are hard to sort. Use them for compost instead of the blue bin.

title: Pizza Boxes
body: If the bottom is greasy, it’s not recyclable. Compost the greasy part and recycle the clean lid.

title: Metal Cans
body: Aluminum and steel cans are 100% recyclable. Rinse them out to avoid attracting pests.

title: No Bubble Wrap
body: Bubble wrap is soft plastic and usually requires a special drop-off point at supermarkets.

title: Compost Scraps
body: Fruit and vegetable peels don't belong in recycling. Start a compost pile for garden soil.

title: Aerosol Cans
body: Ensure aerosol cans are completely empty before placing them in the metal recycling bin.

title: No Styrofoam
body: Styrofoam is rarely accepted in curbside bins. Look for specialized local drop-off centers.

title: Textile Recycling
body: Old clothes shouldn't go in the bin. Donate them or find a dedicated fabric recycler.

title: Don't Wish-cycle
body: If you aren't sure if it's recyclable, throw it out. Non-recyclables ruin the whole load.

title: Steel Jar Lids
body: Remove steel lids from glass jars and recycle them separately in the metal bin.

title: Ink Cartridges
body: Most office supply stores have collection boxes for used printer ink and toner.

title: Milk Cartons (Tetra Pak)
body: These are multi-layered. Ensure your local facility specifically accepts Tetra Paks.

title: No Pyrex Glass
body: Pyrex and ovenware glass have different melting points and contaminate bottle glass.

title: Egg Cartons
body: Paper egg cartons are great for composting or recycling; plastic ones should be cleaned.

title: Junk Mail
body: Remove the plastic windows from envelopes before tossing them into the paper bin.

title: Light Bulbs
body: CFL and LED bulbs contain trace metals. Take them to a hardware store for recycling.

title: Aluminum Foil
body: Clean foil can be recycled. Ball it up until it is at least the size of a tennis ball.

title: Plastic Cutlery
body: Most plastic forks and spoons are too small to be sorted by machines; try to reuse them.

title: Magazine Recycling
body: Glossy magazines are fine to recycle as long as they are not wrapped in plastic.

title: Old Cookware
body: Teflon pans aren't for the bin. Take them to a scrap metal dealer if they are unusable.

title: Garden Waste
body: Keep sticks and grass out of the recycling bin. Use a dedicated green waste bin.

title: Receipt Paper
body: Thermal paper receipts often contain BPA and should be thrown in the trash, not recycled.

title: Mirror Glass
body: Mirrors have a reflective coating that prevents them from being recycled with bottles.

title: Coffee Cups
body: Most takeaway cups have a plastic lining. Recycle the plastic lid, but bin the cup.

title: Empty Hairspray
body: If the can is empty and the plastic cap is removed, it is usually okay for metal recycling.

title: Soft Plastics
body: Bread bags and frozen food bags can often be recycled at grocery store drop-offs.

title: Toothpaste Tubes
body: Most are multi-material and go to trash, unless they are marked as 'Recyclable'.

title: Paint Cans
body: Let leftover paint dry out or use a hardener before taking the can to a hazardous waste site.

title: Hard Drive Security
body: Before recycling old computers, physically destroy or wipe the hard drive data.

title: Window Envelopes
body: Most modern recycling plants can filter out small plastic windows from paper mail.

title: Soap Bottles
body: Rinse out the residue and remove the pump spray, as pumps often contain metal springs.

title: Phone Cases
body: Most silicone or plastic cases aren't recyclable. Look for biodegradable brands.

title: Frozen Food Boxes
body: These often have a thin layer of plastic to prevent freezer burn. Check the label.

title: Wine Corks
body: Natural cork can be composted. Synthetic 'corks' should be thrown in the trash.

title: Old Keys
body: Old metal keys can be taken to a scrap metal recycler rather than the trash.

title: Cereal Boxes
body: Remove the plastic liner inside before flattening and recycling the cardboard box.

title: Used Napkins
body: Paper towels and napkins are usually contaminated by food and should be composted.

title: Spectacles
body: Don't bin old glasses. Many charities collect them to redistribute to people in need.

title: Empty Lighters
body: Disposable lighters are trash. Choose a refillable metal lighter to reduce waste.

title: Plastic Toys
body: Hard plastic toys are rarely recyclable. Donate them to a local charity or school.

title: Pet Food Tins
body: Rinse thoroughly. These are high-quality aluminum or steel and highly recyclable.

title: Bubble Envelopes
body: If you can't separate the paper from the bubbles, the whole item belongs in the trash.

title: Yogurt Containers
body: Clean thoroughly. In many areas, only the container is recyclable, not the foil lid.

title: Berry Containers
body: Clear plastic 'clamshells' are often PET #1 but check if your city accepts this shape.

title: No Ceramic Plates
body: Broken ceramic plates and mugs cannot be recycled with glass. They belong in the trash.

title: Button Batteries
body: Small batteries from watches/hearing aids are hazardous. Store in a jar for e-waste day.

title: Wire Hangers
body: Most curbside programs don't want these. Return them to your local dry cleaners.

title: Coffee Pods
body: Individual pods are hard to recycle. Use a refillable pod or a dedicated brand program.

title: Shampoo Bottles
body: Empty and rinse. If the plastic is marked 1 or 2, it is very easy to recycle.

title: Pizza Dividers
body: That little plastic 'table' in the pizza box is usually recyclable plastic #5.

title: Milk Jug Handles
style: If the jug is HDPE (2), the handle is too. Flatten the jug to save space.

title: Used Motor Oil
body: Never pour oil down the drain. Take it to a local mechanic or hazardous waste site.

title: No Waxed Paper
body: Waxed paper or butcher paper cannot be recycled or composted due to the wax coating.

title: Plastic Film
body: If you can poke your thumb through it (like grocery bags), it's 'soft plastic'.

title: Broken Windows
body: Window pane glass is treated differently than jar glass. Do not put in the blue bin.

title: Old Shoes
body: Worn-out sneakers can sometimes be recycled into playground surfaces by specific brands.

title: Margarine Tubs
body: These are usually plastic #5 (PP). Check the bottom to ensure your facility takes it.

title: Juice Boxes
body: Remove the plastic straw and push it inside the box or throw the straw in the trash.

title: Empty Glue Sticks
body: Once used up, the plastic tube is trash. Look for refillable glue options.

title: Paper Plates
body: Even if they look clean, the fibers are often too short for high-quality recycling.

title: Empty Pill Bottles
body: Remove the label to protect your privacy before recycling the orange plastic bottle.

title: Old CD/DVDs
body: These are made of polycarbonate. They require specialized e-waste recycling centers.

title: Greeting Cards
body: Cards with glitter or foil cannot be recycled. Stick to plain paper cards.

title: Christmas Lights
body: Broken strings of lights are e-waste. Do not put them in your household recycling.

title: Lotion Pumps
body: The pump mechanism is usually trash because of the internal metal spring.

title: Takeout Containers
body: Only recycle if they are free of food grease. Black plastic is often unrecyclable.

title: No Garden Hoses
body: Hoses get tangled in recycling machinery and cause expensive breakdowns.

title: Potato Chip Bags
body: These are shiny because of a plastic/foil mix. They currently belong in the trash.

title: Wine Bottles
body: Remove the foil wrap around the neck before putting the bottle in the glass bin.

title: Vitamins Bottles
body: Glass or plastic vitamin bottles are usually recyclable. Just rinse them first.

title: Dryer Sheets
body: Used dryer sheets are trash. Switch to wool dryer balls to reduce waste.

title: Plastic Buckets
body: Large 5-gallon buckets can be recycled if they are clean and handles are removed.

title: Old Backpacks
body: If the zippers work, donate it. If not, the mixed materials make it trash.

title: Cooking Oil
body: Collect used oil in a jar and take it to a bio-fuel recycling center if available.

title: Hardback Books
body: Remove the hard cover before recycling the inner pages as paper.

title: Cigarette Butts
body: These are the most littered item on earth. They are not recyclable or biodegradable.

title: Soda Cans
body: Aluminum cans have the highest recycling value. Never throw them in the trash.

title: Vegetable Crate
body: Wooden crates can be composted or used for firewood if they aren't painted.

title: Pet Food Bags
body: Large bags often have a plastic liner. If they don't tear like paper, they are trash.

title: Deodorant Sticks
body: Unless they are all-plastic and cleaned, most deodorant tubes go in the trash.

title: Used Tires
body: Retailers usually take old tires when you buy new ones. They can be turned into asphalt.

title: Paper Bags
body: Strong and highly recyclable. They are a great alternative to plastic bags.

title: Microwave Popcorn
body: The bags are coated with chemicals and grease. They are not recyclable.

title: Toothbrushes
body: Bamboo toothbrushes can be composted; plastic ones are generally trash.

title: Calculators
body: Small electronics like these should be treated as e-waste at the end of their life.

title: Laundry Jugs
body: These are sturdy HDPE plastic. Rinse them well and they are very easy to recycle.

title: Plastic Wrap
body: Cling wrap is a 'tangler' for machines. Keep it out of the curbside bin.

title: Gift Wrap
body: If it stays crumpled when you squeeze it, it's paper. If it bounces back, it's plastic.

title: No Diapers
body: Never put diapers in recycling. They are a major biohazard for sorting staff.

title: Ice Cream Tubs
body: The wet-strength paper used for these tubs is usually not recyclable.

title: Empty Bleach
body: As long as it's totally empty and rinsed, the HDPE bottle is recyclable.

title: Think Circular
body: Reducing and Reusing are always more effective than just Recycling!
